title: String Formatting
description: Learn a way to make a String template and insert variable values into it

subtasks.title: Recap
subtasks.explainer:
- What does the `python:len()` **function** do? 
- What are **String methods**?
- What do `python:upper()`, `python:lower()` and `python:title()` do?

subtasks.title: Functions
subtasks.explainer:
**Functions** are pieces of code we can use in our program. They are usually code provided to us to make writing our program easier! 

Some examples are: 
- `python:print()`
- `python:input()`

subtasks.title: Arguments
subtasks.explainer:
We provide information that our **functions** need to run inside the **brackets** ( ). These are called **arguments**. 

For example we provide a String to be shown on the console as an **argument** to the `python:print()` function. 

```python
print("Hello") # Our argument is the "Hello" string!
```

subtasks.title: Return
subtasks.explainer:
**Functions** sometimes **return** a value. 

That means we can store them in a variable or print them straight out: 

```python
print(len("Hello")) # Prints the number 5
```

subtasks.title: Len Function
subtasks.explainer:
The `python:len()` **function** **returns** a number that is the number of **characters** in the string provided as an **argument**. 

```python
print(len("Hello") # This will print the number 5
print(len("Good day") # This will print the number 8
```

subtasks.title: Recap - 1
subtasks.explainer:
Provide a **String** as the **argument** for the `python:len()` **function**. 

See how many **characters** your **String** it is!

subtasks.title: Methods
subtasks.explainer:
A **method** is a **function** that is attached to our **Strings** 

We use them by writing `python:.methodname()` after our String value or **variable**

```python
"Hello".upper()
name = "Ryan"
name.upper()
```

subtasks.title: Not change Variable
subtasks.explainer:
The **methods** we have looked at will not change the value of our **variables**

```python
name = "ryan"
name.title()
print(name) # Still prints "ryan"
# name variable has not changed!
```

subtasks.title: String Methods
subtasks.explainer:
We used three **String methods** last time: 

- `python:upper()` - Makes a String UPPERCASE
- `python:lower()` - Turns a String lowercase
- `python:title()` - Turns a String into Proper Nouns (Capitalised first letter)

subtasks.title: Recap - 2
subtasks.explainer: Print out the `python:saying `**variable **with the .`python:upper()` **method**

incorrectChecks.hint: Make sure to add `python:.upper()` after the `python:saying` **variable** inside `python:print()`

subtasks.title: Recap - 3
subtasks.explainer: Try to `python:print()` the `python:saying` **variable** with the `python:.lower()` **method**.

subtasks.title: Recap - 4
subtasks.explainer:
Create a **variable** called `python:saying` - **Assign** it a **String**. 

`python:print()` that **String** out with the `python:.title()` **method**.

subtasks.title: Recap - 5
subtasks.explainer: Which is the example of a **function** and a **method**?

subtasks.title: Format Method Explained - 1
subtasks.explainer: The `python:.format()` **method** allows us to insert **variables** into our **Strings** super easily!

subtasks.title: Format Method Explained - 2
subtasks.explainer:
It is an alternative to using the `python:+` symbol to combine our **Strings**. 

Do you remember what this is called?

subtasks.title: Format Method Explained - 3
subtasks.explainer:
The `python:.format()` **method** means we don't need to **concatenate** **Strings** to insert **variable values**!

```python
# No more complicated concatenation!
print("Hello " + name + " it is nice to meet you!")
```

subtasks.title: Format Method Explained - 4
subtasks.explainer:
To use the `python:format()` method we need to first create a **template String**. 

This is a **String** that has `python:{}` where we want to insert a **variable value**. 
>:info This is called a **placeholder**

```python
template = "My name is {}" # {} will be replaced by an actual name!
```

subtasks.title: Format Method Explained - 5
subtasks.explainer:
Then we use the `python:.format()` **method** on the **template String variable**. 

We pass in **values** to insert into the **template** as **arguments**. Each **value** will replace the **placeholder** `python:{}` in the **String**. 

Just like the other **String methods** it **returns** a value, so to see it we need to put it inside a `python:print()` **function**. 

```python
template = "My name is {}"
print(template.format("Ryan"))
# This will print "My name is Ryan"

subtasks.title: Format Method Explained - 6
subtasks.explainer:
It is a bit like a **fill in the gaps** quiz. 

We use `python:{}` to setup the gaps. And then we use `python:.format()` to insert the answer!

tasks.title: Use the Format Method
tasks.explainer:
We have a **template String variable** here. 

We are also printing it with the `python:.format()` method. 

Place a **String value** in as an **argument** in the `python:.format()` **method** to use it!

```python
print(template.format("Ryan"))
```

tasks.title: Creating a Template
tasks.explainer:
Try creating your own **template** **variable**.

Change the `python:template` **variable** so that the age `python:5` is replaced by a **placeholder** `python:{}`

tasks.title: Print out the Template
tasks.explainer:
Now add a `python:print()` to use the `.format()` method on the next line. 

Give an age as a **argument** for the `.format()` method. 

```python
print(template.format(11)
```

subtasks.title: Multiple Placeholders
subtasks.explainer:
We can use _multiple_ **placeholders** in a **template String** to add *multiple* **values**. 

We simply use *multiple* `python:{}` inside our **template**. 

```python
template = "My name is {} and I am {} years old"
```

subtasks.title: Multiple Placeholder Explainer - 1
subtasks.explainer:
To insert *multiple* **values** into a **template String** with *multiple* **placeholders** we add more **arguments** seperated by a **comma** - ,

This is like when we use `python:print()` with *multiple* **arguments**!

```python
print("My name is", name)
```

subtasks.title: Multiple Placeholder Explainer - 2
subtasks.explainer:
So to insert **values** into *multiple* **placeholders** we use *multiple* **arguments** seperated by commas - ,

```python
template = "My name is {} and I am {} years old"
print(template.format("Ryan", "12")
# This will print "My name is Ryan and I am 12 years old
```

tasks.title: Using Multiple Placeholders
tasks.explainer:
Use *multiple* **argument** to insert two **values** into the two **placeholders** in the **template String**

```python
print(template.format("Ryan", "12")
```
tasks.starterCode:
template = "My name is {} and I am {} years old!"

print(template.format())
tasks.completeCode:
template = "My name is {} and I am {} years old!"

print(template.format("Ryan", "31"))

check.value: My name is * and I am * years old

tasks.title: Create a template with multiple Placeholders
tasks.explainer:
Create a **template string** with multiple placeholders. 

Change the `python:template` **variable** so that the name `python:Ryan` is replaced by a **placeholder** `python:{}`
tasks.starterCode: template = "My name is Ryan and I am 12 years old"

check.value: My name is {} and I am {} years old

tasks.completeCode: template = "My name is {} and I am {} years old"

tasks.title: Inserting Variables into Placeholders
tasks.explainer:
We can also insert **variables** into the **placeholders** 

```python
template = "My name is {}"
name = "Ryan"
print(template.format(name))
# This will print My name is Ryan

tasks.title: Inserting a Variable into a Placeholder
tasks.explainer:
Create a **variable** called `python:name` and **assign** it a **String value** of your name. 

```python
name = "Ryan"
```

Then add it as an **argument** of the `python:.format()` **method** in the `python:print()` **function**.

```python
print(template.format(name))
```

subtasks.title: Final Task - 1
subtasks.explainer:
Let's combine it together. 

_Multiple_ **Placeholders** and inserting **Variables**.

subtasks.title: Final Task - 2
subtasks.explainer:
First let's write a **template string**. 

```python
template = "{} is the capital city of {}"
```
subtasks.completeCode: template = "{} is the capital city of {}"

subtasks.title: Final Task - 3
subtasks.explainer:
Next let's create two **variables**. 

One for `python:city` and one for `python:country`. **Assign** them each a **value** that makes sense!

```python
city = "London"
country = "England"
```
subtasks.starterCode: template = "{} is the capital city of {}"
subtasks.completeCode:
template = "{} is the capital city of {}"

city = "London"
country = "England"

subtasks.title: Final Task - 4
subtasks.explainer:
Now to `python:print()` the `python:template` **variable** along with the `python:.format()` **method**. 

Be sure to add the **variables** as the **arguments**. 

```python
print(template.format(city, country))
```
subtasks.starterCode:
template = "{} is the capital city of {}"

city = "London"
country = "England"
subtasks.completeCode:
template = "{} is the capital city of {}"

city = "London"
country = "England"

print(template.format(city, country))

subtasks.title: Recap - 1
subtasks.explainer:
- The `python:.format()` **method** can be used to insert **values** into a **String**
- We have to create a **template String** with **placeholders** where we want to insert our **values** - These are `python:{}`

subtasks.title: Recap - 2
subtasks.explainer: What symbols do we use as a **placeholder** in our **template String**?

subtasks.title: Recap - 3
subtasks.explainer:
What would be the **output** of this code? 

```python
template = "My favourite game is {}"
print(template.format("Mario"))
```
